claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) reflects a label-registry encoding where each word is drawn from a small closed vocabulary of astrological terms (< 50 unique words), producing a highly non-uniform unigram distribution unlike the rest of the manuscript.
evidence_for: Zodiac section entropy 3.7149 bits is 0.185 bits below the overall corpus entropy (3.8627 bits), a large and consistent gap
Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios, consistent with a label-heavy format where the same short labels repeat near illustrations
Existing belief [0.396] (confidence 0.396) and [0.111] (confidence 0.111) already associate zodiac low entropy with label-oriented encoding
In standard astrological manuscripts, zodiac sections use repetitive labels for months, constellations, and degree markers â€” a small closed vocabulary is expected
evidence_against: If the zodiac section used only ~50 unique words, its type-token ratio would be ~0.039, far lower than the corpus average; this extreme would likely have been noted in prior analyses
Low entropy alone does not distinguish label repetition from a simpler (less homophonic) cipher variant used in this section
The zodiac section's Zipf behavior has not been independently confirmed to deviate from the rest of the corpus
test: Compute the type-token ratio (TTR) and vocabulary size for the zodiac section alone. Compare the unigram frequency distribution of the zodiac section against the full corpus using Jensen-Shannon divergence. Fit a Zipf curve to the zodiac section word frequencies and compare the exponent to the full-corpus exponent (0.8946). Test whether the top-20 most frequent words in the zodiac section account for > 60% of all zodiac tokens.
test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence between zodiac and full-corpus unigram distributions; Zipf exponent for zodiac section; percentage of zodiac tokens covered by top-20 words; TTR for zodiac section
test_threshold: JS divergence > 0.15 vs. full corpus, Zipf exponent < 0.70 (steeper than corpus), top-20 words covering > 60% of tokens, and TTR < 0.08 would jointly confirm label-registry encoding; failure on two or more metrics would reject this hypothesis

tests_run.test: compute the type-token ratio (ttr) and vocabulary size for the zodiac section alone. compare the uni
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If confirmed, the zodiac section can be treated as a Rosetta-stone-like resource: a short, repetitive label set is the easiest entry point for cipher-breaking, and confirming its closed-vocabulary structure would make it the highest-priority decipherment target rather than the herbal section.
